title: Python Log Analysis & Security Monitoring
description: Master the art of reading, parsing, and analysing security logs with Python to detect threats, anomalies, and incidents.
learning_outcomes: Read and parse common log formats (Apache, auth, syslog, JSON logs)
Use Python regex to extract security-relevant fields from log entries
Build scripts that detect brute-force attacks, privilege escalations, and anomalies
Analyse timestamps and correlate events across multiple log sources
Generate security reports and alerts from log data
Understand log rotation, retention policies, and chain-of-custody
Detect SQL injection, XSS, and path traversal attacks in web server logs
Create statistical baselines and flag deviations indicating compromise
Apply Python data structures to large-scale log processing

theory.introduction:
Every computer system keeps a diary — we call them log files. Just like a shop's CCTV cameras record everyone who walks in and out, log files record every action that happens on a server, network, or application. When something goes wrong — a hacker breaks in, a system crashes, or data gets stolen — the FIRST place investigators look is the logs. They're the digital crime scene evidence.

But here's the problem: modern systems generate MILLIONS of log entries every day. A busy web server might produce 10GB of logs in 24 hours. No human can read all of that. That's where Python comes in. With Python, you can write scripts that automatically read through mountains of log data, extract the important bits, spot patterns that indicate attacks, and raise alerts — all in seconds.

In this module, you'll learn to think like a Security Operations Centre (SOC) analyst. You'll parse real log formats (Apache web server logs, Linux authentication logs, firewall logs, and application JSON logs), build detection rules for common attacks like brute-force login attempts and SQL injection, create timeline correlations across multiple sources, and generate professional security reports.

Think of yourself as a detective: the logs are your witness statements, Python is your magnifying glass, and regex is your fingerprint kit. By the end of this module, you'll be able to turn a wall of text into actionable security intelligence. Let's crack the case!
theory.why_important:
Log analysis is the backbone of cybersecurity defence. According to industry reports, the average time to detect a data breach is over 200 days — and in most cases, the evidence was sitting in log files the entire time, unread and unanalysed. Companies that implement automated log monitoring detect breaches 10x faster.

Every major security framework (NIST, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, GDPR) REQUIRES log monitoring. If you work in cybersecurity — whether as a penetration tester, SOC analyst, incident responder, or security engineer — you WILL read and analyse logs. Python is the most popular language for this because it handles text processing beautifully, has powerful regex support, and can scale from small scripts to enterprise-grade SIEM pipelines.

Beyond professional use, understanding logs helps you protect your own systems. Want to know if someone is trying to hack your server? Check the logs. Want to debug why your application crashed at 3 AM? Check the logs. Logs are the truth — they don't lie, they don't forget, and with Python, they don't stay hidden.

key_concepts.explanation: A log file is a text file where a system automatically records events as they happen. Think of it like a ship captain's log — every event gets a timestamp and description. Servers, applications, firewalls, databases, and operating systems all generate logs. Common locations include /var/log/ on Linux. Each line typically contains: a timestamp (when), a source (who/where), an action (what happened), and a result (success/failure). Understanding log structure is the foundation of all security monitoring.
key_concepts.example:
# Reading a simple log file in Python
with open('server.log', 'r') as f:
    for line_num, line in enumerate(f, 1):
        print(f'Line {line_num}: {line.strip()}')

# Output:
# Line 1: 2025-01-10 14:23:01 INFO User admin logged in from 192.168.1.100
# Line 2: 2025-01-10 14:23:05 WARN Failed login for root from 10.0.0.55
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of log files as your system's diary — every entry has a date, a mood (INFO/WARNING/ERROR), and a story about what happened.

key_concepts.explanation: Different systems use different log formats, just like different countries use different date formats. The most common are: Apache/Nginx Combined Log Format (web servers), Syslog format (Linux system logs), Auth.log format (authentication events), JSON structured logs (modern apps), and CSV/TSV logs (custom applications). Each has its own structure but shares the same DNA: timestamp + source + event + details. Learning to recognise these formats instantly is like a detective learning to read different types of evidence.
key_concepts.example:
# Apache Combined Log Format
apache = '192.168.1.100 - frank [10/Jan/2025:13:55:36 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326'

# Syslog Format
syslog = 'Jan 10 14:23:01 webserver sshd[12345]: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55'

# JSON Log Format
import json
json_log = json.loads('{"timestamp":"2025-01-10T14:23:01Z","level":"ERROR","message":"Invalid token"}')
print(json_log['level'])  # ERROR
key_concepts.ai_hint: Different log formats are like different languages — once you learn the grammar of each one, you can read them all fluently!

key_concepts.explanation: Regular expressions (regex) are patterns that describe text structures. For log analysis, they are your most powerful tool — like a metal detector that finds exactly the needles in the haystack. Python's 're' module lets you define patterns to extract IP addresses, timestamps, usernames, HTTP methods, status codes, and error messages from any log format. A regex like r'(\d{1,3}\.){3}\d{1,3}' matches any IPv4 address. Key components: \d (digit), \s (space), \S (non-space), .* (anything), groups () for capturing, quantifiers + and *.
key_concepts.example:
import re

log = '192.168.1.100 - - [10/Jan/2025:14:23:01 +0000] "GET /admin HTTP/1.1" 403 287'
pattern = r'(\S+) - - \[(.*?)\] "(\S+) (\S+) \S+" (\d{3})'
match = re.search(pattern, log)
if match:
    print(f'IP: {match.group(1)}')        # 192.168.1.100
    print(f'Path: {match.group(4)}')      # /admin
    print(f'Status: {match.group(5)}')    # 403
key_concepts.ai_hint: Regex is like a search template with wildcards on steroids. \d means 'any digit', \S means 'any non-space character', and () captures what you want to keep!

key_concepts.explanation: A brute-force attack is when someone tries thousands of password combinations hoping to guess the right one. In logs, this appears as many failed login attempts from the same IP address in a short time window. Your Python script can detect this by counting failed logins per IP and flagging any IP that exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5 failures in 10 minutes). This is exactly what tools like Fail2Ban do. The analogy: if someone tries 100 different keys on your front door in 5 minutes, that's not a forgetful neighbour — that's a break-in attempt.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Counting failed logins per IP is like counting how many times someone jiggles different keys in your lock — too many tries means suspicious!

key_concepts.explanation: Timestamps are the heartbeat of log analysis. Every event has a 'when', and correlating events by time is how you reconstruct attack timelines. Python's datetime module can parse timestamps from any format into datetime objects, letting you calculate time differences, group events into windows, and detect time-based patterns. 100 requests in 1 second might indicate DDoS, while logins at 3 AM might indicate an attacker in a different timezone. strptime() converts strings to datetime using format codes: %Y=year, %m=month, %d=day, %H=hour, %M=minute, %S=second.
key_concepts.example:
from datetime import datetime

# Parse Apache timestamp
ts = '10/Jan/2025:14:23:01 +0000'
dt = datetime.strptime(ts, '%d/%b/%Y:%H:%M:%S %z')
print(f'Parsed: {dt}')

# Calculate time between events
event1 = datetime(2025,1,10,14,23,1)
event2 = datetime(2025,1,10,14,23,58)
diff = (event2 - event1).total_seconds()
print(f'Gap: {diff} seconds')  # 57.0
key_concepts.ai_hint: strptime = 'string-parse-time'. Match the format codes to your timestamp pattern: %d=day, %b=month-name, %Y=year, %H=hour.

key_concepts.concept: Detecting Web Attacks in HTTP Logs
key_concepts.explanation: Web server logs are a goldmine for detecting attacks. SQL injection appears as requests containing UNION SELECT, ' OR 1=1, DROP TABLE in URLs. XSS shows up as <script> tags or javascript: protocols. Path traversal shows ../ sequences. Your Python script can scan HTTP request paths for these malicious patterns. It's like having a bouncer at a club who checks everyone's ID — except instead of age, you check for attack signatures in URLs.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Attack patterns are like 'red flag words' — your script watches for 'UNION SELECT' and '../' just like spam filters watch for 'FREE MONEY'!

key_concepts.explanation: Raw logs are overwhelming — thousands of similar lines. Aggregation means grouping and counting events to see the big picture. Python's Counter and defaultdict are perfect. Count requests per IP, errors per hour, most-visited pages, status code distribution. This transforms 10,000 lines into a 20-line summary. Like organising 1,000 receipts by category — suddenly you see where the money went. One 404 is normal; 500 from the same IP scanning for vulnerable paths is an attack.
key_concepts.example:
from collections import Counter

logs = ['10.0.0.55 GET /admin 403','10.0.0.55 POST /login 401',
        '10.0.0.55 POST /login 401','192.168.1.1 GET /index 200',
        '192.168.1.1 GET /about 200']

ip_counts = Counter(line.split()[0] for line in logs)
status_counts = Counter(line.split()[-1] for line in logs)

print('Requests per IP:')
for ip, c in ip_counts.most_common():
    print(f'  {ip}: {c}')
print('Status codes:')
for s, c in status_counts.most_common():
    print(f'  {s}: {c}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Counter is like a tally chart — count how many times each thing appears. most_common() gives you the top offenders!

key_concepts.explanation: Batch analysis reviews logs after the fact, but real-time monitoring watches logs as they are written — like live CCTV versus reviewing recordings. Python can tail a log file by seeking to the end and checking for new lines continuously. Combined with pattern matching, your script becomes a live security monitor that alerts the MOMENT something suspicious happens. Uses file.seek(0, 2) to jump to end, then loops with readline(). Adding sleep(0.5) prevents CPU overuse.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Real-time monitoring is like a security guard watching live cameras instead of reviewing yesterday's footage — catch intruders AS they break in!

key_concepts.explanation: Every system has a 'normal' baseline. 500 requests/hour with 2% errors is normal. If suddenly 5,000 requests with 40% errors appear, something is wrong. Anomaly detection means establishing what's normal (mean, standard deviation) and flagging deviations. Values outside 2-3 standard deviations get flagged. This catches zero-day attacks and insider threats that don't match known signatures. Python's statistics module provides mean() and stdev() for building baselines.
key_concepts.example:
import statistics

baseline = [480,520,510,490,505,530,485,515,500,495]
mean = statistics.mean(baseline)
stdev = statistics.stdev(baseline)
threshold = mean + (2 * stdev)

print(f'Normal: {mean:.0f} ± {stdev:.0f}')
print(f'Alert above: {threshold:.0f}')

new_data = [505, 4800, 490, 510]
for val in new_data:
    if val > threshold:
        print(f'ANOMALY: {val} (expected ~{mean:.0f})')
    else:
        print(f'Normal: {val}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Baselines are like knowing your resting heart rate — if it suddenly doubles, something is wrong even if you don't know exactly what!

key_concepts.explanation: The final step is communicating findings. A security report summarises threats, prioritises them, and recommends actions. Python auto-generates reports in text, CSV, HTML, or JSON. A good report includes: executive summary, timeline, affected systems, severity classification, and remediation steps. Think of it as your detective case file — all evidence organised for the security team.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A security report is your case file — clear, organised, actionable. Automate it so you never write one from scratch!

key_concepts.concept: Log Integrity and Chain of Custody
key_concepts.explanation: Logs are digital evidence. Chain of custody means proving they haven't been tampered with — important for legal proceedings. SHA-256 hashing creates a unique fingerprint; even changing one character gives a different hash. Python's hashlib module calculates hashes. Always work on copies, never originals, and document your process. Log rotation (logrotate) compresses old logs (.gz files). Python can read these with gzip.open(). Regulations (GDPR, PCI-DSS) require specific retention periods.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

def hash_file(filepath):
    sha256 = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b''):
            sha256.update(chunk)
    return sha256.hexdigest()

# Create test file
with open('evidence.log', 'w') as f:
    f.write('2025-01-10 Root access from 10.0.0.55\n')

original_hash = hash_file('evidence.log')
print(f'SHA-256: {original_hash[:32]}...')
print('Document this hash before analysis!')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Hashing is like sealing evidence in a tamper-proof bag — if anyone opens it, you'll know. SHA-256 is the gold standard.

key_concepts.explanation: Production logs can be gigabytes. Loading everything with readlines() crashes scripts. Python's line-by-line iteration uses minimal memory. Pre-filter with 'if keyword in line' before expensive regex. Generators yield results one at a time. For compressed files, gzip.open() reads .gz files transparently. Think of it as sifting for gold: coarse filter first (string check), then fine filter (regex) on what remains.
key_concepts.example:
import re, time

def fast_scan(filepath, keyword, pattern):
    matches = []
    total = 0
    start = time.time()
    with open(filepath, 'r', errors='ignore') as f:
        for line in f:
            total += 1
            if keyword not in line:  # Fast pre-filter
                continue
            m = re.search(pattern, line)  # Slow regex only on filtered
            if m:
                matches.append(m.groups())
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    print(f'Scanned {total} lines in {elapsed:.2f}s, found {len(matches)}')
    return matches

print('Two-pass filtering: string check first, regex second')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Processing big files: first remove obvious rocks (keyword filter), then carefully examine what's left (regex)!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Reading and Displaying Log File Contents
complete_examples.description: Opens a log file, reads line by line, displays with line numbers — the fundamental first step.
complete_examples.code:
def read_log_file(filepath):
    entries = []
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8', errors='ignore') as f:
            for num, line in enumerate(f, 1):
                stripped = line.strip()
                if stripped:
                    entries.append((num, stripped))
        print(f'Read {len(entries)} entries from {filepath}')
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print(f'Error: {filepath} not found!')
    return entries

sample = ['2025-01-10 14:23:01 INFO User admin logged in from 192.168.1.100',
          '2025-01-10 14:23:05 WARN Failed login for root from 10.0.0.55',
          '2025-01-10 14:23:10 ERROR Database connection timeout after 30s',
          '2025-01-10 14:23:15 INFO User admin accessed /dashboard',

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Parsing Apache Logs with Regex
complete_examples.description: Uses regex named groups to extract IP, timestamp, method, path, and status from Apache Combined Log Format.
complete_examples.code:
import re

def parse_apache(line):
    pattern = (r'(?P<ip>\S+) \S+ \S+ \[(?P<timestamp>[^\]]+)\] '
               r'"(?P<method>\S+) (?P<path>\S+) (?P<protocol>\S+)" '
               r'(?P<status>\d{3}) (?P<size>\S+)')
    match = re.match(pattern, line)
    return match.groupdict() if match else None

logs = [
    '192.168.1.100 - frank [10/Jan/2025:13:55:36 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326',
    '10.0.0.55 - - [10/Jan/2025:13:56:01 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401 185',
    '10.0.0.55 - - [10/Jan/2025:13:56:02 +0000] "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401 185',
    '10.0.0.55 - - [10/Jan/2025:13:56:03 +0000] "GET /admin HTTP/1.1" 403 287',
    '10.0.0.55 - - [10/Jan/2025:13:56:15 +0000] "GET /../../etc/passwd HTTP/1.1" 400 0',
    '192.168.1.200 - - [10/Jan/2025:13:56:20 +0000] "GET /products?id=1 HTTP/1.1" 200 1847',
]

for i, log in enumerate(logs, 1):
    p = parse_apache(log)
    if p:
        icon = '🚨' if int(p['status']) >= 400 else '✅'
        print(f'Entry {i}: {icon}  IP:{p["ip"]}  {p["method"]} {p["path"]}  Status:{p["status"]}')

Entry 2: 🚨  IP:10.0.0.55  POST /login  Status:401
Entry 3: 🚨  IP:10.0.0.55  POST /login  Status:401
Entry 4: 🚨  IP:10.0.0.55  GET /admin  Status:403
Entry 5: 🚨  IP:10.0.0.55  GET /../../etc/passwd  Status:400
Entry 6: ✅  IP:192.168.1.200  GET /products?id=1  Status:200
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Named groups (?P<n>...) make code readable. re.match() checks from string start. groupdict() returns all groups as a dict. Status codes >= 400 indicate errors/blocked requests — exactly how SOC analysts triage.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Brute-Force Login Detection
complete_examples.description: Counts failed SSH login attempts per IP using Counter and flags IPs exceeding the threshold.
complete_examples.code:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import re

auth_logs = [
    'Jan 10 14:23:01 sshd: Accepted password for admin from 192.168.1.100',
    'Jan 10 14:23:05 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:06 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:07 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:08 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:09 sshd: Failed password for admin from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:10 sshd: Failed password for admin from 10.0.0.55',
    'Jan 10 14:23:15 sshd: Accepted password for dev from 192.168.1.200',
    'Jan 10 14:23:20 sshd: Failed password for test from 10.0.0.99',
    'Jan 10 14:23:25 sshd: Failed password for test from 10.0.0.99',
    'Jan 10 14:23:30 sshd: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.55',
]

def detect_brute_force(logs, threshold=5):
    failed_by_ip = Counter()
    targeted = defaultdict(set)
    for line in logs:
        if 'Failed password' in line:
            ip = re.search(r'from (\S+)', line).group(1)
            user = re.search(r'for (\S+)', line).group(1)
            failed_by_ip[ip] += 1
            targeted[ip].add(user)
    print('=== BRUTE-FORCE DETECTION ===')
    for ip, count in failed_by_ip.most_common():
        users = ', '.join(targeted[ip])
        flag = '🚨 FLAGGED' if count >= threshold else '✅ OK'
        bar = '█' * count
        print(f'  {ip:15s} | {bar} ({count}) → {flag}  [users: {users}]')

detect_brute_force(auth_logs, threshold=5)
complete_examples.output:
=== BRUTE-FORCE DETECTION ===
  10.0.0.55       | ███████ (7) → 🚨 FLAGGED  [users: root, admin]
  10.0.0.99       | ██ (2) → ✅ OK  [users: test]
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Counter counts failed logins per IP. defaultdict(set) tracks unique targeted usernames. The bar chart makes patterns visible at a glance. This is exactly how Fail2Ban works internally.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add time window detection: only flag IPs with 5+ failures within 60 seconds by parsing the timestamps.

    '{"ts":"14:23:35","level":"INFO","service":"backup","msg":"Backup completed","size_gb":2.3}',
]

complete_examples.title: Example 7: IP Address Analyser
complete_examples.description: Categorises IPs as internal/external, checks against a blocklist, and builds activity profiles.
complete_examples.code:
import re
from collections import defaultdict

PRIVATE_RANGES = [re.compile(r'^10\.'), re.compile(r'^172\.(1[6-9]|2\d|3[01])\.'), re.compile(r'^192\.168\.')]
BLOCKLIST = {'10.0.0.55', '203.0.113.66'}

def classify_ip(ip):
    for pat in PRIVATE_RANGES:
        if pat.match(ip): return 'Internal'
    return 'External'

logs = ['192.168.1.100 GET /index 200', '10.0.0.55 POST /login 401',
        '10.0.0.55 POST /login 401', '10.0.0.55 GET /admin 403',
        '172.16.5.20 GET /dashboard 200', '203.0.113.66 GET /../../etc/passwd 400',
        '192.168.1.200 GET /products 200']

ip_data = defaultdict(lambda: {'count':0, 'actions':[]})
for line in logs:
    parts = line.split()
    ip = parts[0]
    ip_data[ip]['count'] += 1
    ip_data[ip]['actions'].append(f'{parts[1]} {parts[2]} → {parts[3]}')

print('=== IP ANALYSIS ===')
for ip in sorted(ip_data):
    d = ip_data[ip]
    cat = classify_ip(ip)
    blocked = '🔴 BLOCKED' if ip in BLOCKLIST else ('🟢 '+cat if cat=='Internal' else '🟡 '+cat)
    print(f'\n  {ip} [{blocked}] — {d["count"]} requests')
    for a in d['actions'][:3]:
        print(f'    → {a}')

=== IP ANALYSIS ===

  10.0.0.55 [🔴 BLOCKED] — 3 requests
    → POST /login → 401
    → POST /login → 401
    → GET /admin → 403

  172.16.5.20 [🟢 Internal] — 1 requests
    → GET /dashboard → 200

  192.168.1.100 [🟢 Internal] — 1 requests
    → GET /index → 200

  192.168.1.200 [🟢 Internal] — 1 requests
    → GET /products → 200

  203.0.113.66 [🔴 BLOCKED] — 1 requests
    → GET /../../etc/passwd → 400
complete_examples.ai_explanation: RFC 1918 defines private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x). Regex patterns match these prefixes. The blocklist simulates a threat intelligence feed. defaultdict builds profiles per IP. In production, blocklists come from services like AbuseIPDB.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Automated Security Report Generator
complete_examples.description: Collects analysis findings and generates a formatted report with executive summary, findings, and recommendations.
complete_examples.code:
from datetime import datetime

class ReportGenerator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.threats = []
        self.recs = []
    def add_threat(self, sev, cat, desc, ip, evidence):
        self.threats.append({'sev':sev,'cat':cat,'desc':desc,'ip':ip,'ev':evidence})
    def add_rec(self, priority, action):
        self.recs.append({'p':priority,'a':action})
    def generate(self):
        r = ['='*55, '   SECURITY LOG ANALYSIS REPORT',
             f'   Generated: {datetime.now():%Y-%m-%d %H:%M}', '='*55]
        crit = sum(1 for t in self.threats if t['sev']=='CRITICAL')
        high = sum(1 for t in self.threats if t['sev']=='HIGH')
        r.append(f'\n  EXECUTIVE SUMMARY')
        r.append(f'  Threats: {len(self.threats)} | Critical: {crit} | High: {high}')
        r.append(f'  Risk Level: {"CRITICAL" if crit else "HIGH" if high else "MEDIUM"}')
        r.append(f'\n  FINDINGS')
        for i, t in enumerate(sorted(self.threats, key=lambda x: ['CRITICAL','HIGH','MEDIUM','LOW'].index(x['sev'])), 1):
            r.append(f'  #{i} [{t["sev"]}] {t["cat"]} from {t["ip"]}')
            r.append(f'     {t["desc"]}')
        r.append(f'\n  RECOMMENDATIONS')
        for rec in sorted(self.recs, key=lambda x: x['p']):
            r.append(f'  [{rec["p"]}] {rec["a"]}')
        r.append('='*55)
        return '\n'.join(r)

rg = ReportGenerator()
rg.add_threat('CRITICAL','SQL Injection','UNION SELECT payload detected','10.0.0.55','GET /search?q=UNION SELECT...')
rg.add_threat('HIGH','Brute-Force','47 failed logins in 5 minutes','10.0.0.55','47x Failed password for root')
rg.add_threat('HIGH','Path Traversal','Attempted /etc/passwd access','203.0.113.66','GET /file?name=../../etc/passwd')
rg.add_rec('P1','Block 10.0.0.55 at firewall immediately')
rg.add_rec('P1','Check database for data exfiltration')
rg.add_rec('P2','Enable WAF SQL injection rules')
rg.add_rec('P3','Review directory permissions')
print(rg.generate())

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Failed Login Rate Calculator
complete_examples.description: Calculates failed login rates per minute, builds a histogram, and detects rate spikes indicating attacks.
complete_examples.code:
from collections import defaultdict

def analyse_rates(logs):
    per_minute = defaultdict(int)
    per_ip = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(int))
    for line in logs:
        parts = line.split()
        ts, result, ip = parts[0], parts[1], parts[-1]
        minute = ts[:5]  # HH:MM
        if result == 'FAILED':
            per_minute[minute] += 1
            per_ip[ip][minute] += 1
    print('=== FAILURE RATE PER MINUTE ===')
    for m in sorted(per_minute):
        c = per_minute[m]
        bar = '█' * c
        alert = ' 🚨 SPIKE!' if c >= 5 else ''
        print(f'  {m} | {bar} ({c}){alert}')
    print('\n=== PER IP PER MINUTE ===')
    for ip in sorted(per_ip):
        print(f'  {ip}:')
        for m in sorted(per_ip[ip]):
            print(f'    {m} | {"▓" * per_ip[ip][m]} ({per_ip[ip][m]})')

logs = [
    '14:20:01 SUCCESS admin 192.168.1.100',
    '14:20:15 FAILED root 10.0.0.55', '14:20:16 FAILED root 10.0.0.55',
    '14:20:17 FAILED admin 10.0.0.55', '14:20:18 FAILED root 10.0.0.55',
    '14:20:19 FAILED root 10.0.0.55', '14:20:20 FAILED test 10.0.0.55',
    '14:20:21 FAILED root 10.0.0.55',
    '14:21:01 FAILED user1 10.0.0.99', '14:21:05 FAILED user2 10.0.0.99',
    '14:22:01 FAILED root 10.0.0.55', '14:22:02 FAILED root 10.0.0.55',
    '14:22:03 FAILED root 10.0.0.55',
]
analyse_rates(logs)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Time-bucketing groups events by minute to see WHEN attacks happen. defaultdict(int) counts per bucket. The nested defaultdict creates per-IP-per-minute analysis. Visual bars make spikes obvious. This is the foundation of rate-limiting systems.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Log File Integrity Checker
complete_examples.description: Uses SHA-256 hashing to verify log file integrity for forensic chain-of-custody.
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, os, json

def hash_file(path):
    sha = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b''):
            sha.update(chunk)
    return sha.hexdigest()

# Create evidence file
with open('evidence.log', 'w') as f:
    f.write('2025-01-10 Root access from 10.0.0.55\n')
    f.write('2025-01-10 Data exported to 10.0.0.55\n')

# Register hash
original = hash_file('evidence.log')
print(f'1. Registered: SHA-256={original[:40]}...')

# Verify (unchanged)
current = hash_file('evidence.log')
print(f'2. Verify: {"✅ INTACT" if current == original else "🚨 TAMPERED"}')

# Simulate tampering
with open('evidence.log', 'w') as f:
    f.write('2025-01-10 Root access from 10.0.0.55\n')  # Removed line!

tampered = hash_file('evidence.log')
print(f'3. After tampering: {"✅ INTACT" if tampered == original else "🚨 TAMPERED"}')
print(f'   Expected: {original[:30]}...')
print(f'   Got:      {tampered[:30]}...')

os.remove('evidence.log')
complete_examples.output:
1. Registered: SHA-256=a1b2c3d4...
2. Verify: ✅ INTACT
3. After tampering: 🚨 TAMPERED
   Expected: a1b2c3d4...
   Got:      x7y8z9w0...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: SHA-256 creates a unique fingerprint — change even one character and the hash is completely different. Register the hash BEFORE analysis, verify AFTER. This proves evidence wasn't tampered with. Critical for legal proceedings.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add MD5 as a secondary hash and create a verify_all() function that checks multiple files.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Anomaly Detection with Statistical Baseline
complete_examples.description: Builds a statistical baseline from historical data and detects anomalies using standard deviation.
complete_examples.code:
import statistics, random

random.seed(42)
historical = [random.gauss(500, 30) for _ in range(100)]
mean = statistics.mean(historical)
stdev = statistics.stdev(historical)
upper = mean + 2*stdev
lower = mean - 2*stdev

print(f'Baseline: mean={mean:.0f}, stdev={stdev:.0f}')
print(f'Normal range: {lower:.0f} - {upper:.0f}')

new_data = [505, 510, 4800, 490, 520, 15, 500, 495, 510, 480, 530, 490]
print('\n=== MONITORING ===')
anomalies = 0
for i, val in enumerate(new_data, 1):
    if val > upper:
        print(f'  Hour {i:2d}: {val:5.0f} 🚨 HIGH (possible DDoS)')
        anomalies += 1
    elif val < lower:
        print(f'  Hour {i:2d}: {val:5.0f} 🚨 LOW (possible outage)')
        anomalies += 1
    else:
        print(f'  Hour {i:2d}: {val:5.0f} ✅')

print(f'\nAnomalies: {anomalies}/{len(new_data)}')
complete_examples.output:
Baseline: mean=500, stdev=30
Normal range: 440 - 560

=== MONITORING ===
  Hour  1:   505 ✅
  Hour  2:   510 ✅
  Hour  3:  4800 🚨 HIGH (possible DDoS)
  Hour  4:   490 ✅
  Hour  5:   520 ✅
  Hour  6:    15 🚨 LOW (possible outage)
  ...

Anomalies: 2/12
complete_examples.ai_explanation: 95% of normal values fall within 2 standard deviations. Values outside are anomalies. A high spike (4800) = possible DDoS. A low dip (15) = possible outage. This catches attacks that don't match known signatures.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add time-of-day awareness: what's normal at 2 PM might be anomalous at 3 AM.

visual_diagram.title: Python Log Analysis Pipeline — Complete Visual Flowchart
visual_diagram.content:
🔍 LOG ANALYSIS PIPELINE FLOWCHART
📂 START: Raw Log Files
▼
STAGE 1: INGEST & PARSE
├── 📄 Open log file with open(filepath, 'r')
├── 🔄 Read line by line (memory efficient)
├── 🔤 Identify format: Apache? Syslog? JSON?
├── 🎯 Apply regex pattern to extract fields
│ ├── IP address (who)
│ ├── Timestamp (when)
│ ├── Action/method (what)
│ ├── Path/resource (where)
│ └── Status/result (outcome)
└── 📦 Store as dict: {ip, time, action, status}
▼ Parsed entries
STAGE 2: THREAT DETECTION
├── 🔍 Scan each entry against attack signatures:
│ ├── 💉 SQL Injection: union select, ' OR '1'='1
│ ├── 🕷️ XSS: <script>, javascript:, onerror=
│ ├── 📁 Path Traversal: ../, ../../etc/passwd
│ ├── ⌨️ Command Injection: ; cat, | whoami
│ └── 🔐 Brute Force: multiple 401/403 same IP
├── 📊 Count failed logins per IP (Counter)
└── 🚨 Flag IPs exceeding threshold
▼ Threats identified
STAGE 3: CORRELATION & ANALYSIS
├── 🔗 Correlate events by IP across log sources
│ ├── Same IP in web + auth = multi-vector attack
│ └── Timeline reconstruction with sorting
├── 📈 Statistical baseline comparison
│ ├── Calculate mean and standard deviation
│ ├── Flag values > 2 StdDevs from normal
│ └── Detect DDoS spikes and outage dips
├── 🗂️ Aggregate: requests/IP, errors/hour
└── 🏷️ Classify: CRITICAL → HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW
▼ Analysis complete
STAGE 4: REPORT & RESPOND
├── 📋 Generate report: summary + findings + recommendations
├── 🚨 Trigger alerts for CRITICAL findings
├── 🔒 Recommend: block IPs, patch vulns, update rules
└── 💾 Save report + evidence with SHA-256 hash
✅ PIPELINE COMPLETE
🔑 KEY PYTHON TOOLS

📥 INGEST: open(), re.match(), json.loads(), gzip.open()
🔍 DETECT: re.compile(), re.search(), Counter(), 'in' operator
📊 ANALYSE: statistics.mean(), stdev(), defaultdict(), sorted()
📋 REPORT: string formatting, json.dump(), hashlib.sha256()
💡 REMEMBER

🕵️ Logs = WITNESS STATEMENTS — each line tells part of the story
🔍 Regex = FINGERPRINT KIT — extract the evidence you need
📊 Counters = TALLY CHART — spot who does what, how often
📅 Timestamps = TIMELINE — reconstruct the sequence
🔗 Correlate = CONNECT CLUES from different witnesses
📈 Baselines = NORMAL heartbeat — flag deviations
🔒 Hash everything = PROVE evidence wasn't tampered with
⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES

❌ Loading entire file with readlines() — use line-by-line for big files
❌ Using regex without pre-filtering — string check first, regex second
❌ Ignoring timezones — always normalise timestamps
❌ Hardcoding thresholds — make them configurable
❌ Working on originals — always copy forensic evidence first
❌ Forgetting error handling — logs can be malformed

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Multi-Metric Dashboard

hints.message: For each metric, compute baseline from hist, check new values.
Loop metrics.items(), compute mean/stdev per metric.

def dashboard(metrics):
    import statistics
    print('=== SECURITY DASHBOARD ===')
    for name,(hist,new) in metrics.items():
        m=statistics.mean(hist); s=max(statistics.stdev(hist) if len(hist)>1 else 1,1)
        hi,lo=m+2*s,m-2*s
        vals=[f'{v} {"🚨" if v>hi or v<lo else "✅"}' for v in new]
        print(f'{name+":":12s} {" | ".join(vals)}')

hints.message: Hash each file, record hash + size, save inventory.
for each file: compute hash, get size, add to inventory list

def package_evidence(files):
    import hashlib,os,json; inv=[]
    print(f'=== EVIDENCE PACKAGE ===\nFiles: {len(files)}')
    for fp in files:
        sha=hashlib.sha256()
        with open(fp,'rb') as f:
            for c in iter(lambda:f.read(8192),b''): sha.update(c)
        h=sha.hexdigest(); sz=os.path.getsize(fp)
        inv.append({'file':fp,'sha256':h,'size':sz})
        print(f'  {fp}: sha256={h[:16]}... ({sz} bytes)')
    with open('evidence_inventory.json','w') as f: json.dump(inv,f,indent=2)
    print('Inventory saved to evidence_inventory.json')
